summary: Global markets are displaying a cautiously optimistic mood, with stocks staging a rally even as oil prices decline, though underlying tensions tied to the ongoing Iran war continue to shape sentiment across asset classes and geographies. The dominant macro driver remains the Iran conflict, which is disrupting Strait of Hormuz shipping lanes through Iranian checkpoints and informal "fees," pressuring global energy supply chains, while the Bank of England signals it has room to monitor the war's economic spillover before adjusting policy and the UAE accelerates a Hormuz-bypass pipeline now 50% complete ahead of a 2027 launch. At the sector and stock level, Nvidia is acting as a key market stabilizer during equity dips, Europe is seeing a notable surge in EV sales as petrol prices rise from the conflict, Russia's oil and gas revenues jumped 39% year-over-year in May as a direct beneficiary of the war, and airlines including Qatar Airways are reporting significant profit hits from rerouting and disruption costs. The primary risk factors investors are watching include Trump's ambiguous threat to strike Iran harder, bond market pressure that appears to have already caused a policy pivot — with Reuters noting Trump "blinked" as yields rose — alongside inflationary pressure from energy costs hitting UK consumers, European farmers, and diesel-dependent industries globally. In the short term, markets are likely to remain volatile and headline-driven, with oil price direction, any escalation or diplomatic progress on Hormuz, and bond market stability serving as the critical variables determining whether the current equity rally has staying power.
